failures.truthValue: human-reviewed Raul Menendez read on reviewed segments
human-reviewed as David on these segments
deeper squadmate voice, grounded in-scene delivery

The trailer’s dialogue sets up Raul Menendez as the puppet-master behind fear, unrest, and psychological manipulation, while David and his squad are pulled through a surreal hallucinatory mission shaped by trauma, memory, and control. Corporate propaganda, military urgency, squad confusion, Menendez taunts, and Master of Puppets lyrics all reinforce the same theme: people are being manipulated like puppets. The dialogue arc ends with David choosing to confront that force directly, before the trailer shifts into a preorder promo.
failures.outputValue: A dramatic trailer featuring dialogue about control, fear, and conflict. A female narrator speaks about manipulation, while a villain threatens violence. Military and personal stakes are established through exchanges between a conflicted protagonist named David, a mentor figure, and antagonist Menendez. The trailer concludes with a promotional call-to-action for a pre-order bonus.

Speaker registry (human-reviewed on the listed cod-test truth segments; uncertain identities stay uncertain):
- spk_001 => Speaker 1 | grounded confidence: 0.92 | acoustic cues: urgent female office/corporate delivery | inferred traits: gender: female (human-reviewed opening speaker read) | recent lines: Menendez is a terrorist. / We're bringing peace and security to the world.
- spk_002 => Speaker 2 | grounded confidence: 0.90 | acoustic cues: menacing, deep, gravelly antagonist delivery | inferred traits: gender: male (human-reviewed Raul Menendez read on reviewed segments) | recent lines: Your streets, shall once again run red with your blood. / So eager to leave daddy.
- spk_003 => Speaker 3 | grounded confidence: 0.90 | acoustic cues: authoritative male expository/briefing delivery | inferred traits: gender: male (human review supports an authoritative male delivery, but identity remains uncertain: could be the older African-American general/leader or a separate one-off expository speaker, with not enough confidence to merge yet) | recent lines: Raul Menendez ignited global unrest on an unprecedented scale.
- spk_006 => Speaker 6 | grounded confidence: 0.88 | acoustic cues: distressed younger male character voice | inferred traits: gender: male (human-reviewed as David on these segments) | recent lines: A lot of people counting on us for answers. / No more games! This ends now.
- spk_007 => Speaker 7 | grounded confidence: 0.90 | acoustic cues: authoritative, gravelly, older military-leader cadence | inferred traits: gender: male (human-reviewed older general/leader read) | recent lines: Stop looking backwards, David. What matters is what we do next.
- spk_008 => Speaker 8 | grounded confidence: 0.88 | acoustic cues: younger male comms/radio voice | inferred traits: gender: male (human review now effectively confirms the same younger male comms speaker across both lines; an African-American read is supported by the audible "rahport" pronunciation cue on "report", but that remains a read rather than hard fact) | recent lines: Specter one, report. / Need a sitrep.
- spk_009 => Speaker 9 | grounded confidence: 0.86 | acoustic cues: deeper squadmate voice, grounded in-scene delivery | inferred traits: gender: male (human review read this as a deeper African-American male squadmate voice) | recent lines: This isn't real.
- spk_010 => Speaker 10 | grounded confidence: 0.86 | acoustic cues: angry, frustrated white male squadmate delivery | inferred traits: gender: male (human review explicitly ruled out David) | recent lines: The hell it ain't!
- spk_011 => Speaker 11 | grounded confidence: 0.92 | acoustic cues: sung heavy-metal lyric vocal | inferred traits: gender: male (Master of Puppets vocalist / music lyric, not in-scene dialogue) | recent lines: Obey your master! / Master, master
- spk_012 => Speaker 12 | grounded confidence: 0.88 | acoustic cues: urgent adult squadmate command | inferred traits: gender: female (human review read an African-American female squadmate with tattoos) | recent lines: Pull it together, man!
- spk_013 => Speaker 13 | grounded confidence: 0.85 | acoustic cues: white male-sounding montage voice tied to Frank Woods visual imagery | inferred traits: gender: male (identity remains uncertain; do not collapse into Raul or David) | recent lines: Killing the man is a hell of a lot easier than killing the idea.
- spk_014 => Speaker 14 | grounded confidence: 0.85 | acoustic cues: older male voice with robotic/processed filter | inferred traits: gender: male (antagonist-coded; possibly Raul Menendez but identity intentionally left uncertain) | recent lines: You shall know fear.
- spk_015 => Speaker 15 | grounded confidence: 0.80 | acoustic cues: mixed overlapping voices with female-primary layer | inferred traits: gender: ambiguous (male and female layers overlap; primary audible layer resembles Speaker 1) | recent lines: You were never cut out to be a Mason.
- spk_016 => Speaker 16 | grounded confidence: 0.88 | acoustic cues: white adult male sales/promo voiceover | inferred traits: gender: male (promo VO, distinct from scene dialogue and lyrics) | recent lines: Get the Reznov challenge pack when you preorder now!

Voice separation reminders:
- speaker_id continuity is acoustic, not semantic.
- Do not collapse scene dialogue, comms, sung lyrics, promo VO, and overlapping hallucination blends into one speaker unless the voice clearly matches.
- If identity is uncertain, preserve the uncertainty in notes/descriptors instead of promoting a guess to fact.
- The mixed "You were never cut out to be a Mason" line is intentionally represented as an overlap-heavy speaker bucket rather than a falsely precise identity claim.

Corporate propaganda, military urgency, squad confusion, Menendez taunts, and Master of Puppets lyrics all reinforce the same theme: people are being manipulated like puppets. The dialogue arc ends with David choosing to confront that force directly, before the trailer shifts into a preorder promo.

Military and personal stakes are established through exchanges between a conflicted protagonist named David, a mentor figure, and antagonist Menendez. The trailer concludes with a promotional call-to-action for a pre-order bonus.
